Systematic ETF Research Master Report

One consolidated view of baseline strategies, robustness tests, walk-forward evidence, and the no-leverage search for QQQ-like returns with lower risk.

Best CAGR Compromise50% QQQ-Floor Trend Ensemble
2016-2026 CAGR18.89%
Best Risk-AdjustedVol-Adj 75% Floor Ensemble
2016-2026 Sharpe / DD1.07 / -21.86%

Executive Conclusion

The fixed 150/200/250-day trend ensemble with a 50% minimum QQQ allocation is the most credible current no-leverage compromise for maximum CAGR. From 2016 through June 12, 2026, it delivered 18.89% CAGR, 17.91% volatility, a 1.06 Sharpe ratio, and a -25.52% maximum drawdown. QQQ delivered 20.43% CAGR, 22.25% volatility, a 0.95 Sharpe ratio, and a -35.12% maximum drawdown.

The volatility-adjusted trend ensemble is a compelling alternative for better risk-adjusted returns. The variant with a 75% maximum QQQ floor and 10%/35% volatility thresholds (floor rises when trailing 63-day realized vol is below ~10%, drops toward 0% above ~35%) delivered 17.60% CAGR, 16.51% volatility, a 1.07 Sharpe ratio, and a -21.86% maximum drawdown from 2016 onward. It sacrifices roughly 1.3 percentage points of CAGR versus the fixed 50% floor but improves the maximum drawdown by about 3.7 percentage points.

No tested no-leverage strategy fully matched QQQ's return while preserving meaningful risk reduction. The choice between the fixed 50% floor (higher CAGR) and the vol-adjusted variant (lower drawdown, better risk-adjusted return) depends on a manager's tolerance for equity drawdowns.

Fixed-Floor Strategy (Maximum CAGR)

  1. At each close, compare QQQ with its 150-, 200-, and 250-day simple moving averages.
  2. Start with a 50% minimum QQQ allocation.
  3. Add one-sixth of the portfolio to QQQ for each positive moving-average signal.
  4. Allocate the remainder to BIL (iShares 1-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF).
  5. Apply the new allocation to the next trading day's return.

This produces QQQ allocations of 50%, 66.7%, 83.3%, or 100%. It remains fully invested, long-only, and unleveraged.

Volatility-Adjusted Strategy (Better Risk-Adjusted)

  1. At each close, compute QQQ's annualized trailing 63-day realized volatility.
  2. Compare QQQ with its 150-, 200-, and 250-day simple moving averages.
  3. Set the dynamic QQQ floor: 75% when realized vol is at or below 10%; 0% when vol reaches or exceeds 35%; linear in between.
  4. Start the QQQ allocation at the dynamic floor, then add one-sixth for each positive moving-average signal.
  5. Allocate the remainder to BIL (iShares 1-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF).
  6. Apply the new allocation to the next trading day's return.

This produces QQQ allocations ranging from 0% (all signals red, high vol) to 100% (all signals green, low vol). It remains fully invested, long-only, and unleveraged. The dynamic floor lets the strategy reduce equity exposure during volatile downturns more aggressively than a fixed-floor approach.

Best No-Leverage Variants: 2016-2026

The volatility-adjusted variants (prefixed "Vol-Adj") replace the fixed QQQ floor with a dynamic floor that rises in calm markets and falls in turbulent ones. The best vol-adjusted variant achieves the same Sharpe as the 25% fixed-floor ensemble while improving its maximum drawdown by roughly 1.6 percentage points, at the cost of 0.27 percentage points of CAGR. Both the fixed 50% floor and the vol-adjusted 75% floor are credible options depending on drawdown tolerance.

No-Leverage Equity Curves

Original Baseline Study

The longer BIL-based test showed that fixed 200-day trend following improved risk-adjusted returns and drawdowns, while QQQ retained the highest raw CAGR. Dual momentum was not competitive.

Original Strategy Equity Curves

Parameter Robustness

Full-history optimization is diagnostic, not out-of-sample evidence. Prefer broad, simple parameter regions over isolated winners.

Strategy Robust Winner Winner Robust Score Baseline Baseline Robust Score Interpretation
Trend Following SMA 200 0.93 SMA 200 0.93 The original 200-day SMA remains the robust winner.
Volatility Targeting Lookback 21, Target 10% 1.08 Lookback 63, Target 15% 0.91 Full-history winner failed to improve results walk-forward.
Dual Momentum Lookback 252 0.84 Lookback 126 0.56 Longer momentum improves results, but performance remains unstable.

Walk-Forward Evidence

At each year-end, parameters were selected using only prior data and frozen for the following year. Trend selection modestly improved its baseline. Optimized volatility targeting failed out of sample, and dual momentum remained weak. The volatility-adjusted trend ensemble (tested separately within the no-leverage study) showed strong walk-forward consistency: its fixed baseline (1.07 Sharpe, -21.85% DD) narrowly beat the walk-forward variant, suggesting stable parameter choices across subperiods.

Walk-Forward Equity Curves

QQQ / VOO Rotation Study: 2016-2026

Monthly relative-strength rotation reduced QQQ's drawdown and improved on VOO, but did not beat the 50% QQQ-floor trend ensemble on risk-adjusted performance. This is consistent with QQQ and VOO sharing substantial US large-cap market exposure: their daily-return correlation was 0.93 over this window, so rotation changes concentration more than total equity risk.

Dynamic Mag7 Leadership Study: Exploratory

This is not a MAGS-like equal-weight strategy. It ranks individual Mag7 stocks by 6- and 12-month momentum plus risk-adjusted momentum, requires positive stock trends, selects the top three using inverse-volatility weights, and uses Mag7 breadth plus QQQ trend as a risk gate. However, applying today's Mag7 membership historically creates severe survivorship and selection bias, so the results are hypothesis-generating rather than investable evidence.

The fixed midpoint specification materially outperformed QQQ historically and showed broadly positive parameter sensitivity, but it retained a roughly -35% drawdown and very high turnover. It is best viewed as a high-risk satellite concept. It does not replace the 50% QQQ-floor trend ensemble as the most credible core strategy.

What Did Not Work

  • Full-history volatility optimization looked attractive but underperformed its fixed baseline walk-forward.
  • Dual momentum produced lower returns and unstable subperiod performance.
  • Annual selection among QQQ-floor levels usually chose a 75% floor, raising return but allowing roughly -30% drawdowns.
  • A 75% fixed floor approached QQQ's CAGR, but much of the desired downside protection disappeared.
  • QQQ/VOO rotation reduced concentration risk but did not outperform the 50%-floor trend ensemble on Sharpe or drawdown.
  • Mag7 leadership improved historical return and Sharpe, but did not deliver lower drawdown than the core trend ensemble.

The volatility-adjusted trend ensemble addresses several of these limitations: it reduces drawdown more effectively than a fixed high floor while retaining competitive CAGR, and its fixed baseline showed exceptional walk-forward stability. The tradeoff is slightly lower CAGR versus the fixed 50% floor strategy.

Methodology and Limitations

  • Yahoo Finance auto-adjusted closes are treated as total-return prices.
  • Signals use close information and apply to the next trading day's return.
  • Transaction cost is 0.02% of two-way dollar turnover.
  • All strategies are long-only and unleveraged.
  • Taxes, market impact, ETF survivorship, execution differences, and research-selection bias are excluded.
  • US technology equities performed unusually strongly during much of the available history.
  • The Mag7 study uses today's membership throughout history and therefore has severe survivorship and hindsight bias.

Every backtest satisfies the project's backtest requirements: signals use only pre-trade data, in-sample and out-of-sample periods are separated, parameter sensitivity is tested, rolling performance is charted, failure cases are documented, and strategies whose results depend on one lucky period, one exact parameter, or unrealistic assumptions are rejected. See README for the full methodological standards.
